---
title: "Contingency Tables"
---

<span class="section-badge">Basics</span>

When both your variables are **categorical**, the appropriate tools come from the chi-square family. A **contingency table** (cross-tabulation) shows the frequency of every combination of categories, and a chi-square test asks whether the two variables are independent.

This page covers:

- [Chi-square test of independence](#chi-square-test-of-independence) — are two categorical variables related?
- [Chi-square goodness-of-fit](#chi-square-goodness-of-fit) — does a single categorical variable match an expected distribution?
- [Fisher's exact test](#fishers-exact-test) — the small-sample alternative

---

## Chi-square test of independence

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## Research vignette — do sexualised images of female athletes shape women's self-evaluations?
Daniels (2012) randomly assigned women to see either sexualised or performance-focused images of female athletes, then coded each participant's open-ended response for whether it expressed *self-objectification* (yes/no). Two categorical variables — image type (2 levels) × self-objectification (2 levels) — gives a 2 × 2 contingency table; the chi-square test of independence asks whether the image manipulation actually pushed women into a different way of talking about themselves. Source: Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 19).

→ Worked through in [Exercise 1 below](#exercise-1-chi-square-does-image-type-sexualised-vs-performance-shape-womens-reactions-to-female-athletes).
:::

### What is it?

The chi-square test of independence asks: **are two categorical variables related, or independent?** It compares the *observed* cell frequencies in a contingency table against the *expected* frequencies under the null hypothesis of independence.

$$\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}$$

**Example**: do voting preferences (Party A / Party B / Party C) differ across age groups (18–30, 31–50, 51+)? The contingency table is 3 × 3; the null hypothesis is that age group and party preference are independent.

### When to use it

- Both variables are **categorical** (nominal or ordinal treated as nominal)
- Each participant contributes to **exactly one cell** of the table (one row per participant)
- You want to test whether membership in one variable's categories is related to membership in the other's

### Assumptions

::: {.assumption-list}
- **Independence**: each observation is independent of the others (no participant appears in more than one cell)
- **Expected cell counts**: all expected counts should be ≥ 5 (a common rule of thumb); at minimum, no more than 20% of cells should have expected counts below 5
- If expected counts are too small, use **Fisher's exact test** instead (see below)
:::

### Effect size

Report **Cramér's V** (or **φ** for a 2 × 2 table):

| Cramér's V | Conventional label (df = 1) |
|---|---|
| .10 | Small |
| .30 | Medium |
| .50 | Large |

For larger tables, the benchmarks scale down — consult DSUJ Chapter 19 or use JASP's effect size output.

### How to do it in JASP

Go to **Frequencies → Contingency Tables** in the analysis menu.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## JASP Video Library — Contingency Tables
Recordings at [jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Frequencies.html](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Frequencies.html):

- [▶ Main Analysis](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Frequencies.html#contingency-main-analysis) — setting up the table, requesting chi-square
- [▶ Cells](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Frequencies.html#contingency-cells) — observed counts, expected counts, residuals
- [▶ Statistics](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Frequencies.html#contingency-statistics) — Cramér's V and other effect sizes
:::

### Interpreting the output

| Statistic | What it means |
|---|---|
| **χ²** | The test statistic — bigger means observed frequencies deviate more from expected |
| **df** | (rows − 1) × (columns − 1) |
| **p** | Reject H₀ (independence) if *p* < α |
| **Cramér's V** | Effect size — the strength of association |

If the test is significant, inspect the **standardised residuals** to see which cells contribute most to the deviation from independence.

::: {.callout-tip}
## APA reporting example
*A chi-square test of independence showed a significant association between age group and party preference, χ²(4) = 18.6, p < .001, Cramér's V = .24. Inspection of the standardised residuals indicated that voters aged 51+ were over-represented among Party A supporters.*
:::

---

## Chi-square goodness-of-fit

A variant of the chi-square test for **one categorical variable** against a hypothesised distribution. Example: does a die roll uniformly (each face expected at 1/6) over 600 throws?

In JASP: **Frequencies → Multinomial Test**. Specify either equal expected probabilities or a custom expected distribution.

---

## Fisher's exact test

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## Research vignette — Fisher's "lady tasting tea"
The historical origin of the test, from R. A. Fisher's *The Design of Experiments* (1935): a lady (Muriel Bristol) claimed she could tell whether the milk had been poured into the cup before or after the tea. Fisher prepared 8 cups, 4 each way, in random order. With expected cell counts well below 5 in every cell, the standard chi-square approximation is unreliable — so Fisher derived the *exact* test by enumerating all possible cup-arrangements with the same row and column totals. The same logic applies any time you've got a 2 × 2 table from a small sample.
:::

When expected cell counts are too small for the chi-square approximation to be trustworthy (typically a 2 × 2 table with any expected count < 5), Fisher's exact test computes the *exact* probability of the observed table under the null. It is available in the same JASP dialog — just tick **Fisher's exact test** under *Statistics*.

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Why Fisher's exact, not chi-square, with small expected counts
The chi-square statistic only *approximately* follows the χ² distribution; the approximation breaks down when expected counts are small. Fisher's exact test side-steps the approximation altogether by enumerating every possible table with the same row and column totals. It costs nothing in interpretation — you still report a *p*-value — and is the safer choice for sparse tables.
:::

---

## Further reading

- **DSUJ Chapter 19** — *Categorical data* — chi-square, Fisher's exact, and odds ratios with JASP walkthroughs
- JASP blog — [How to interpret a contingency table](https://jasp-stats.org/2017/10/04/contingency-tables-jasp/)

---

## Further viewing — SSR lecture recording

The full 2026 SSR lecture covers the chi-square family in depth (captions are available in the SharePoint player):

- [▶ SSR Lecture 28 — Chi-Square](https://amsuni-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/j_b_vandoorn_uva_nl/EUdZ0iYtIZBLsOyakIXtcJMBDfwT9iC1FrMlmUpiIYHsdQ?nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJTdHJlYW1XZWJBcHAiLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJTaGFyZURpYWxvZy1MaW5rIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXcifX0%3D&e=uArm3T)

---

## Try it yourself

Each exercise below ends with a collapsible solution. Try the analysis first, then expand to compare.

### Exercise 1 (chi-square) — Does image type (sexualised vs performance) shape women's reactions to female athletes?

Daniels (2012) showed women either sexualised or performance-focused images of female athletes, then coded each participant's open-ended response for whether it expressed **self-objectification** (yes/no). Using [`leni_16a.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_16a.jasp), run a chi-square test of independence on image type × self-objectification. Inspect the **standardised residuals** to see which cells drive the result.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Labcoat Leni — The impact of sexualised images on women's self-evaluations](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/labcoat_leni#the-impact-of-sexualized-images-on-womens-self-evaluations)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [leni_16a.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_16a.html)
:::

### Exercise 2 (chi-square × three outcomes) — Are perceptions of happiness associated with the professional group rated?

Three groups of professionals rated each other on three different happiness-related measures. Using [`leni_16b.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_16b.jasp), run a chi-square test for **each** of the three outcomes. Report Cramér's V and discuss whether you should adjust for multiple testing.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Labcoat Leni — Is the Black American happy?](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/labcoat_leni#is-the-black-american-happy)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [leni_16b.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_16b.html)
:::

### Exercise 3 (chi-square on a large sample) — Does time-pressure at work affect off-hours psychological detachment?

Sonnentag (2012) surveyed 1709 employees on their work time-pressure (5 ordered levels) and whether they could psychologically *detach* from work in their off-hours. Using [`sonnentag_2012.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_data/data/sonnentag_2012.jasp), set up the contingency table with `time_pressure` in *Rows*, `detachment` in *Columns*, and `frequency` in *Counts*. Run the chi-square test, report Cramér's V, and inspect the standardised residuals to see which levels of time-pressure drive the association.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Smart Alex Task 16.1](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/smart_alex#task-16.1)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [alex_16_01.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_alex/alex_16_01.html)
:::
